    Subcluster Formation

    Subcluster Formation

    Type:Intermediate / specialist concept
    Context:Early stellar cluster assembly
    Related: Star cluster formation
    Fractal structure of star clusters
    Stellar feedback
    Dynamical relaxation
    Q parameter (fractality)

    Subclusters are local stellar overdensities within a forming or young star cluster — smaller groups of stars that formed from the same parent molecular cloud but in distinct spatial locations. Subcluster formation is a natural outcome of the hierarchical, fractal nature of star cluster assembly from turbulent molecular clouds.12

    Formation Mechanisms

    Two primary mechanisms for subcluster formation have been identified in MHD+N-body simulations3:

    • Feedback-triggered: A massive star forms in a primary cluster and expels surrounding gas via photoionization or stellar winds. The expelled gas sweeps up into a dense shell, which becomes gravitationally unstable and collapses to form a secondary subcluster at the edge of the feedback region. This was observed in model n3s6 of Akhmetali et al. (2026)3.
    • Dynamical (proximity merging): Two subclusters form in close proximity and begin to merge under their mutual gravitational attraction. After merging into a single system, dynamical interactions can cause the system to re-fragment into two or more subclusters. This results in oscillating Q values (merging → Q rises; separation → Q falls) and was observed in models n3s7 and n4s1.3

    Observational Evidence

    Substructure in young star clusters is well-established observationally. Kuhn et al. (2014)2 identified subclusters in Chandra observations of massive star-forming regions. Sun et al. (2022)4 found hierarchical subcluster structure in the W40 and Serpens South region. Bastian et al. (2009)5 traced the spatial evolution of stellar structures in the LMC. Some clusters retain substructure well beyond 100 Myr (e.g., NGC 1513, NGC 16416).

    Effect on Cluster Fractality

    The presence and dynamics of subclusters directly control the evolution of the Q parameter:

    • When subclusters are present and spatially separated, Q is low (fractal).
    • During merging events, the stellar distribution momentarily concentrates, raising Q.
    • Re-separation lowers Q again, producing oscillations.
    • In simulations where subcluster activity persists (via feedback or repeated merging), fractality can be maintained beyond 4 tff.3

    Stochasticity

    Feedback-triggered subcluster formation is not universal. Of 13 simulations with the same initial conditions but different random seeds, only 3 developed persistent subclusters.3 The outcome depends critically on whether a massive star forms and when and where it forms relative to dense gas concentrations — an inherently stochastic process driven by the IMF sampling.

    See Also

    • Star cluster formation
    • Fractal structure of star clusters
    • Stellar feedback
    • Q parameter
    • Dynamical relaxation

    References
    1. Vázquez-Semadeni, E. et al. (2017). "Hierarchical star cluster assembly in globally collapsing molecular clouds." MNRAS, 467, 1313–1328.
    2. Kuhn, M. A. et al. (2014). "The spatial structure of young stellar clusters. I. Subclusters." ApJ, 787, 107.
    3. Akhmetali, A. et al. (2026). "Evolution of fractality in centrally concentrated young clusters." A&A (submitted). arXiv:2603.16183
    4. Sun, J. et al. (2022). "Hierarchical structure of YSO clusters in the W40 and Serpens South region." MNRAS, 516, 5258–5269.
    5. Bastian, N. et al. (2009). "The spatial evolution of stellar structures in the LMC." MNRAS, 392, 868–878.
    6. Sánchez, N. & Alfaro, E. J. (2009). "The spatial distribution of stars in open clusters." ApJ, 696, 2086.
    7. Laverde-Villarreal, E. et al. (2025). "The Evolution of Substructure during Star Cluster Assembly." ApJ, 989, 22.
